title: Dog Ate Sago Palm? Immediate Emergency Triage Guide
meta_desc: If your dog ate a Sago Palm, it is a life-threatening emergency. Learn why immediate veterinary care is vital and how to identify the risks.
content_html:
What is Sago Palm?

The Sago Palm (Cycas revoluta) is an ornamental plant frequently used in landscaping and as a houseplant. Despite its name, it is actually a cycad. Every part of the plant, including the fronds and roots, is highly toxic to dogs, but the seeds (or nuts) contain the highest concentration of the toxin cycasin.

Is it an Emergency?

Urgency Level: High/Critical. Yes, ingestion of any part of a Sago Palm is a life-threatening emergency. Even a very small amount can lead to acute liver failure, permanent organ damage, or death. You must contact an emergency veterinarian or a pet poison control hotline immediately. Do not wait for symptoms to appear, as internal damage often begins before your dog looks sick.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

When you call or arrive at the clinic, having a photo of the plant and the specific parts your dog may have eaten is incredibly helpful. There are many types of palms, and some are non-toxic; a clear photo allows the veterinary team to confirm the species and the severity of the exposure. This speeds up the triage process and ensures your dog receives the correct life-saving treatment without delay.

faqs.question: What are the early symptoms of Sago Palm poisoning?
faqs.answer: The first signs typically include vomiting, drooling, and loss of appetite. These may appear within minutes or hours of ingestion.

faqs.question: How much Sago Palm is toxic to dogs?
faqs.answer: There is no safe amount. Ingesting just one or two seeds can be fatal for a medium-sized dog.

faqs.question: What is the survival rate for Sago Palm ingestion?
faqs.answer: The survival rate is approximately 50% with aggressive medical treatment. Success depends heavily on how quickly the dog is seen by a veterinarian.
